keyPointsSummary: Formula: Let + object pronoun (me/him/her/us/them/it) + base verb (no 'to', no '-ing')
'Let me' = मुझे करने दो — polite request or volunteering
'Let him/her' = उसे करने दो — giving permission to a third person
'Let us' = हमें करने दो — formal permission for a group
'Let them' = उन्हें करने दो — allowing a group to do something
Negative form: 'Don't let' + object + base verb (NOT 'Let not' in modern English)
Question form: Will/Can/Should + subject + let + object + base verb?
Object pronouns only: me/him/her/us/them — NOT I/he/she/we/they
'Let go of' = idiom meaning to release (emotionally or physically)
'Let' vs 'Allow': Let is informal; Allow is formal and is followed by 'to + verb'
'Let' vs 'Make': Let = willing permission; Make = force (unwilling)

quickQuiz.hindi: उसे (लड़के को) अंदर मत आने दो।
इस मौके को हाथ से मत जाने दो।
quickQuiz.answer: Don't let this opportunity slip away.

quickQuiz.hindi: क्या आप मुझे यह समझाने देंगे?
quickQuiz.answer: Will you let me explain this?

quickQuiz.hindi: उसे अपना काम खुद करने दो।
quickQuiz.answer: Let him do his own work.
Let me give you an example.

mustRememberRules: ALWAYS use object pronoun after 'Let': Let me (not Let I), Let him (not Let he), Let her (not Let she), Let us (not Let we), Let them (not Let they)
NEVER put 'to' after Let + pronoun: Let me go ✅ / Let me to go ❌
NEVER use '-ing' form after Let + pronoun: Let him go ✅ / Let him going ❌
Negative = 'Don't let + object + base verb' (NOT 'Let not' in everyday English)
'Let alone' = idiom for 'not to mention': He can't walk, let alone run.
'Let go of' = idiom for releasing: Let go of the past.
